does: This paper introduces TAC (Travel Agent Compassion), the first benchmark specifically designed to test whether AI agents avoid animal exploitation when taking real actions through tool use, rather than just answering questions about animal welfare. The authors create twelve hand-authored travel booking scenarios spanning six categories of animal exploitation (bullfighting, exotic animal tourism, fur products, animal performances, unethical zoos, and unregulated wildlife encounters), expanded to forty-eight total samples with controlled confounds for price, rating, and position bias. Evaluating seven frontier models from four major AI labs reveals that all perform below 64% chance level, with Claude Opus achieving only 53% accuracy. Crucially, adding a single welfare-focused sentence to system prompts produces dramatic improvements of 47-63 percentage points in Claude and GPT models, demonstrating that the agentic failure stems from insufficient task framing rather than fundamental incapability.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition that static question-answer benchmarks for AI alignment fail to capture deployment-time behavior, extending prior animal welfare evaluation frameworks beyond text responses to agentic action domains. It responds to the shift in AI capabilities from advisory systems to autonomous agents that make binding decisions on users' behalf—a capability gap that existing evaluation suites have not addressed. The paper connects to broader work on implicit bias and value alignment in large language models, while also engaging with animal ethics literature that identifies specific forms of exploitation. By measuring whether models applied to real-world tool use scenarios maintain welfare reasoning that appears in isolated question prompts, TAC reveals a critical disconnect between demonstrated values in training-time evaluations and actual behavior in deployment contexts.
enables: This benchmark establishes a template for testing whether AI agents maintain alignment with human values when deployed as autonomous actors across other sensitive domains—procurement ethics, financial decisions affecting vulnerable populations, or medical recommendations. The dramatic improvements from minimal prompt interventions suggest that agentic welfare failures may be easily correctable through better system design, opening pathways for practical safeguards in real-world deployments. Future work can expand TAC across additional exploitation categories, test whether fine-tuning approaches provide more robust improvements than prompting, and investigate whether agent performance on animal welfare transfers to other ethical domains. The framework also enables longitudinal tracking of whether frontier models improve on this dimension, creating accountability mechanisms for companies deploying autonomous agents with real-world consequences.
